title: What Can Chickens Eat? Complete Safety Guide

rows.benefits: Excellent high-protein treat; great for molting hens needing extra protein to regrow feathers.
rows.caution: Limit to 10% of diet; live mealworms even better.

rows.benefits: Seeds act as a natural de-wormer; flesh is hydrating and rich in beta-carotene.
rows.caution: Offer raw or cooked; seeds can be fed whole.

rows.benefits: Highly hydrating; great summer treat. Chickens love pecking at the pink flesh.
rows.caution: Remove rind for easier eating; high sugar so limit portions.

rows.benefits: Rich in vitamins A, C, K; kale is a flock favorite. Hang leaves for enrichment.
rows.caution: Spinach has oxalates — rotate with other greens rather than feeding exclusively.

rows.benefits: Good source of vitamin C and antioxidants. Chickens go wild for the red color.
rows.caution: Feed as occasional treat due to natural sugar content.

rows.benefits: Antioxidant-rich superfood. Small size makes them ideal treats that chickens can grab easily.
rows.caution: Fine as a regular treat in moderation.

rows.benefits: Excellent protein boost. Especially helpful during molt or for underweight birds.
rows.caution: Always cook first — raw eggs can encourage egg-eating behavior.

rows.benefits: Provides probiotics that support gut health and digestion. Hens love the creamy texture.
rows.caution: Use plain, unsweetened only. Limit to a few tablespoons per bird.

rows.benefits: Warming winter treat. Good source of energy, vitamins, and minerals.
rows.caution: Cooked is easier to digest. Avoid sugary instant oatmeal packets.

rows.benefits: High energy; helps maintain body heat in winter. Chickens love cracked corn.
rows.caution: High in carbs — limit in summer or for overweight birds.

rows.benefits: High in protein and vitamins. Frozen peas are a popular cooling summer treat.
rows.caution: Feed thawed, not frozen solid. Avoid canned peas with added salt.

rows.benefits: Nutrient-dense; rich in vitamins C and K, calcium, and fiber.
rows.caution: Hang as a treat ball for enrichment. Feed raw or lightly cooked.

rows.benefits: Natural antibiotic properties; supports immune function. Popular herbal addition to feed.
rows.caution: Can be added directly to feed or scattered in coop.

rows.benefits: Anti-inflammatory properties; rich in antioxidants. Chickens enjoy fresh leaves.
rows.caution: Feed freely — no significant cautions.

rows.benefits: Natural insect repellent when scattered in nesting boxes. Refreshing treat in summer.
rows.caution: Plant in containers to prevent invasive spreading in the yard.

rows.benefits: Good source of potassium and B vitamins. Most chickens love the soft texture.
rows.caution: Remove peel (too tough and may have pesticide residue). High in sugar — limit.

rows.benefits: Good source of fiber and vitamins. Chickens enjoy sliced apple as a treat.
rows.caution: Remove seeds and core — apple seeds contain small amounts of cyanide compounds.

rows.benefits: High in protein and healthy fats; great for feather quality and winter energy.
rows.caution: Offer unsalted only. Fine as a regular supplement.

rows.benefits: Natural foraging food; high protein and chickens love hunting them. Great behavioral enrichment.
rows.caution: From clean soil only — avoid worms from areas treated with pesticides or herbicides.

rows.benefits: Easy to digest; good energy source. Useful for birds recovering from illness.
rows.caution: Plain only — no salt, butter, or seasonings. Avoid uncooked rice in large amounts.

rows.benefits: Chickens enjoy pecking at noodles. Provides carbohydrate energy.
rows.caution: Plain, unseasoned only. Treat only — low in nutrients compared to feed.

rows.benefits: Highly hydrating; cool summer treat. Chickens enjoy pecking at halved cucumbers.
rows.caution: Slice or halve for easy access. No significant cautions.

rows.benefits: Rich in vitamins A and C. Winter squash seeds offer similar benefits to pumpkin seeds.
rows.caution: Can be fed raw or cooked. Seeds are safe and enjoyed.

rows.benefits: Rich in beta-carotene which enhances egg yolk color. Chickens enjoy shredded or cooked carrots.
rows.caution: Grate or cook whole carrots for easier eating. Hard raw carrots may be ignored.

rows.benefits: Ripe tomatoes are safe and enjoyed. Good source of lycopene and vitamins.
rows.caution: Only ripe red tomatoes — green/unripe tomatoes contain solanine. Remove stems and leaves.

rows.benefits: Chickens are omnivores and will eat cooked meat readily. Good protein source.
rows.caution: Plain, unseasoned only. No skin or fatty scraps in large amounts.

rows.benefits: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids; supports feather quality and overall health.
rows.caution: Remove all bones. Plain only — no salt, sauces, or seasonings.

rows.benefits: High in protein and calcium; calcium supports strong eggshells.
rows.caution: Offer sparingly — high fat and dairy can cause loose droppings in excess.

rows.benefits: Excellent source of beta-carotene, vitamins, and energy. Hens love the soft texture.
rows.caution: Cook thoroughly. Raw sweet potato is harder to digest.

rows.benefits: Great enrichment — hang a whole cabbage for pecking fun. Nutritious and hydrating.
rows.caution: Can cause gas in very large amounts. Fine in normal treat quantities.

rows.benefits: Rich in antioxidants and vitamin C. Small size makes them easy, no-prep treats.
rows.caution: Fine as regular treats in moderation.

rows.benefits: Chickens love grapes; rich in natural sugars for quick energy.
rows.caution: Halve to prevent choking. High sugar — limit to a few per bird.

rows.benefits: Not toxic, but most chickens avoid citrus due to strong scent.
rows.caution: High acid content may reduce calcium absorption and interfere with laying. Limit or avoid.

rows.benefits: Nutritious but risky.
rows.caution: Can encourage egg-eating behavior in the flock. Always cook eggs before feeding.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Chickens cannot handle high sodium. Salt toxicity can cause excessive thirst, kidney damage.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: No nutritional benefit; can cause obesity and displace nutritious feed. Avoid entirely.

rows.benefits: Chickens enjoy it but it is nutritionally empty.
rows.caution: Minimal only — fills crop without nutrition. Moldy bread is toxic (never feed mold).

rows.benefits: Mostly water; low nutritional value compared to dark leafy greens.
rows.caution: Large amounts cause watery droppings. Prefer romaine, kale, or chard instead.

rows.benefits: None — do not feed.
rows.caution: Contains persin — a fungicidal toxin that causes myocardial necrosis and death in birds.

rows.benefits: None — do not feed.
rows.caution: Theobromine and caffeine are toxic to chickens. Even small amounts can be fatal.

rows.benefits: None — do not feed.
rows.caution: Contain thiosulfates that cause hemolytic anemia in poultry. All alliums (garlic in large amounts) carry risk.

rows.benefits: Small amounts (crushed in water) may support immune function.
rows.caution: Large amounts can cause hemolytic anemia similar to onions. Small supplemental doses only.

rows.benefits: None when raw.
rows.caution: Contains phytohemagglutinin (PHA) — a lectin that is highly toxic to chickens. Always cook beans thoroughly before offering.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Contain solanine, a glycoalkaloid toxic to chickens. Only ripe red tomatoes are safe.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Part of the nightshade family — all green parts contain solanine. Keep tomato plants fenced away from the flock.

rows.benefits: None when green or raw.
rows.caution: Green skin and sprouts contain solanine. Cooked, peeled potato is safe in small amounts.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Leaves contain very high levels of oxalic acid, which is toxic and potentially fatal. Stalks also have some oxalic acid.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Entire nightshade plant (leaves, unripe berries) is toxic. Keep chickens away from nightshade plants in the garden.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Mold produces mycotoxins that damage the liver and immune system. Never feed any food showing mold.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Caffeine is toxic to birds; can cause heart arrhythmia. Keep coffee grounds out of the compost if chickens access it.

rows.benefits: None.
rows.caution: Toxic even in very small amounts. Never offer fermented foods intended for human consumption.

note: This guide covers common foods only. When in doubt, leave it out. Always ensure clean fresh water is available — especially when feeding dry or salty treats.
